findings.title: Sequential CSRF+POST timeouts can compound to ~30s wait
findings.why_it_matters: updatePrStatus() sequentially awaits _csrf() (an independent _fetchWithTimeout call with its own fresh 15s timer) before starting the main POST's own independent 15s _fetchWithTimeout call, so if the backend is slow/hanging on both endpoints a single button click (mark won/skipped) can leave the user waiting up to ~30s before any feedback appears -- longer than the 15s the B3 fix intends as the outer bound for 'stuck on this page' on an interactive, single-click action. This is not a hang (still bounded, a strict improvement over the pre-fix unbounded wait) but the compounding is concrete and always-exercised in production: _csrf() has no module-level token cache (unlike client.ts's cached `_csrf` variable) and the SPA shell is served as a static file with no Jinja templating, so no `<meta name="csrf-token">` tag is ever present to short-circuit the fetch -- every updatePrStatus call pays the full CSRF round-trip before the status POST even starts.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider capping the combined worst-case latency for a single interactive action, e.g. a shorter timeout for the CSRF hop specifically, caching the CSRF token at module scope (mirroring client.ts's `_csrf` variable, refetched only on a 403) so most clicks skip the CSRF round-trip entirely, or fetching the token once eagerly on page mount rather than per-click.
evidence: frontend/src/api/prQueue.ts:69-77 -- `updatePrStatus` builds its POST call with `'X-CSRFToken': await _csrf()` inline in the headers object, so `_csrf()`'s own `_fetchWithTimeout` (up to 15s) fully resolves before the main POST's `_fetchWithTimeout` (another up to 15s) even starts -- sequential, not parallel, worst case ~30s.

frontend/src/api/prQueue.ts:41-52 -- `_csrf()` has no module-level cache of the resolved token (unlike frontend/src/api/client.ts:21,37-38's `let _csrf: string | null = null` pattern), so every call re-fetches when no `<meta>` tag is present.

webui_app/routes/spa.py:38-53 -- the SPA shell is served via `send_from_directory(..., 'index.html')`, a static file with no Jinja templating, confirming no server-rendered `<meta name="csrf-token">` tag exists in the `/app/pr-queue` page to short-circuit `_csrf()`'s network fetch (matches the rationale already documented in frontend/src/api/client.ts's file header comment).

residual_risks: PrQueuePage.vue's markStatus() catch block (unchanged by this diff) discards the actual updatePrStatus failure reason and just calls load() again ('Error is surfaced by load()'); if that reload succeeds the user gets zero indication their status-update click failed. This is pre-existing and out of scope for B3/B4/B5, but B3's new 15s timeout turns what used to be an indefinite hang on a bad update into a concrete rejection that now flows straight into this silent-swallow path, making it more likely to be hit in practice.

prQueue.ts's hand-rolled _fetchWithTimeout deliberately does not retry on transient network/AbortError (unlike client.ts's withRetry, 1 retry), so /api/pr-queue and /api/pr-queue/status remain less resilient to a single transient blip than every other SPA endpoint routed through client.ts -- an intentional, documented scope-narrowing decision (code comment explains it), but a durable behavioral asymmetry worth being aware of.

The health-bar dismiss recheck (B5) fully closes the specific race it targets: because the fetchJson('/health').then() callback runs synchronously from its dismiss-check through the classList mutations with no awaited yield point in between, a concurrent dismiss click cannot interleave mid-callback under JS's single-threaded run-to-completion model -- this narrows to zero, not just narrows, for this exact interaction.
testing_gaps: Neither the new prQueue.spec.ts AbortError tests nor client.spec.ts's existing equivalent exercise the real `setTimeout(..., 15_000)` firing -- both substitute an immediately-thrown DOMException for the mocked fetch instead of using fake timers to advance past the real window, so the actual timer -> ctrl.abort() wiring inside _fetchWithTimeout is never executed by the suite (a regression that broke that wiring, e.g. an uncleared/mis-scoped timer, would not be caught).

test_health_bar_fetch_resolution_rechecks_dismiss_before_showing only asserts the dismiss-check string literal appears twice in the source (a static string-count assertion) rather than exercising the actual race with a controlled/deferred fetch promise and a simulated dismiss click in between -- a change that duplicated the guard string elsewhere without it being on the actual execution path inside .then() could still pass.

The new B4 test in PrQueuePage.spec.ts only covers two DIFFERENT rows' markStatus()-triggered load() calls overlapping; it does not add coverage for a manual '刷新' click racing a markStatus()-triggered reload (both call the same load(), so the generation-counter guard is likely origin-agnostic, but that specific interleaving remains untested).
